name: Degrees of Freedom Analysis
summary: Degrees of Freedom (DOF) analysis is a systematic method for determining how many independent motions a robotic mechanism can make. It is the starting point for kinematic design — determining whether a robot can reach the required positions and orientations, and whether it has the right level of dexterity for the task.
whenToUseIt: Use DOF analysis at the very beginning of any robot design process. Before selecting motors, sensors, or controllers, you must determine how many independent motions the robot needs. DOF analysis answers questions like: Can a 4-DOF arm reach every point in the required workspace? Does a mobile platform need omnidirectional wheels or are two driven wheels sufficient? Is the gripper mechanism over-constrained (too few DOF) or under-constrained (redundant DOF that add cost and complexity)?
steps: Define the task requirements: What positions and orientations must the end effector reach? A pick-and-place task on a flat table may only need 4 DOF (x, y, z, and rotation around vertical). A welding task on a complex 3D surface needs 6 DOF (full position and orientation control).

Apply the Grubler-Kutzbach formula: For planar mechanisms, DOF = 3(n-1) - 2j, where n is the number of links (including the ground) and j is the number of single-DOF joints. For spatial mechanisms, DOF = 6(n-1) - 5j1 - 4j2 - 3j3 - 2j4 - j5, where jN is the number of joints with N constraints.

Verify against the task: Compare the mechanism's DOF with the task requirements. If DOF equals the task DOF, the mechanism is exactly constrained. If DOF exceeds the task DOF, the mechanism is redundant — it has extra flexibility but also extra complexity. If DOF is less than the task DOF, the mechanism cannot reach all required configurations.

Consider workspace: DOF alone does not guarantee reachability. A 6-DOF arm with short links may have the right number of DOF but insufficient reach. Analyse the workspace (set of all reachable positions and orientations) to ensure it covers the task requirements.

Evaluate singularities: Identify configurations where the mechanism loses an effective DOF — these are singularities. Near singularities, the mechanism's performance degrades (infinite joint velocities needed for small end-effector motions). Design the workspace and task layout to avoid singularities during normal operation.

Decide on redundancy: If the task benefits from extra flexibility (obstacle avoidance, singularity avoidance), consider adding a DOF beyond the minimum. Seven-DOF arms are common precisely because the extra DOF provides valuable configuration flexibility.
example: An Australian agricultural robotics company is designing a robotic arm for pruning grapevines in the Barossa Valley. The pruning tool must reach any point on the vine canopy (3 DOF for position) and orient the cutting blade perpendicular to the branch (2 DOF for orientation — rotation around the branch axis does not matter). DOF analysis shows 5 DOF are needed. The team designs a 6-DOF arm (one redundant DOF) to provide extra flexibility for navigating around trellis wires and neighbouring vines. They verify the workspace covers the typical canopy volume (1.5m wide x 2m tall) and identify two singularity configurations to avoid in the control software.
